message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM LIFE INSURANCE products — comprehensive product reference. Cover: (1) the full current term lineup and product names (e.g. the "G" guaranteed level-premium series Term 10-G / 15-G / 20-G / 30-G, form series ICC18-20522, and any other current term products like LSW Level Term / annually-renewable term); (2) issue ages and available level term lengths; (3) the level-premium period then annually-renewable-to-age-95 structure, whether the policy has cash value/dividends (it does not); (4) the conversion privilege — window (first 20 years or to age 70 if earlier), no evidence of insurability, equivalent rate class, what permanent products it converts to; (5) the Accelerated Benefit Rider (ABR) living-benefit suite available on term (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) — cost (no additional premium), triggers, discounted/condition-specific payout, and per-insured lifetime benefit limits and how they vary by state; (6) premium structure and rate classes (Elite/Preferred/Select/Standard/Express, tobacco/non-tobacco); (7) state availability variations; (8) foreign-national / non-US-resident rules for term (minimum face, US nexus/solicitation/delivery requirements, whether Brazil/"B" country is eligible, whether term is even offered to foreign nationals). Prioritize NLG/LSW primary sources: nationallife.com, the Life Product Quick Reference Chart, agent guides, product guides, rider brochures, and the Foreign National Guidelines. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates (which change often). Produce a cited report with source URLs.

## Claim under review
"LSW's level-term lineup in this guide consists of seven level-premium, level-benefit products — fully guaranteed 10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G plus 15-NG, 20-NG, 30-NG (guaranteed 10 years, current-level thereafter) — with coverage on all plans extending to age 95."

**Source:** http://theinsgroup.net/images/stories/LSW/Life/LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Life Insurance Company of the Southwest is pleased to bring you seven level premium, level benefit term life products... We have fully guaranteed level premium plans of 10, 15, 20 and 30 years, as well as 15, 20, and 30-year level premium plans with a 10-year guarantee."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
I'm examining a claim about a specific product guide document that lists seven level-premium term life products with different guarantee periods and coverage extending to age 95. The supporting evidence does mention these products, but I need to identify potential gaps or contradictions in how the claim characterizes them—particularly around the guarantee structures and whether all products truly offer the same age-95 coverage.

The bigger issue is that this appears to be describing an older product lineup that's no longer current. The research indicates the present term lineup consists of just four G-series products (10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G), not the seven-product mix including NG variants mentioned in the claim. So while the guide content itself may be accurately quoted, the claim presents a discontinued product structure as if it were the active offering.
I need to pull up the source document and look through it to find what's currently listed.

input.prompt: What are the exact term product names and lineup? Does it say "seven level premium, level benefit term life products"? What are the product durations/guarantees? Does coverage extend to age 95? Are products named with "-G" or "-NG" suffixes? What is the document date, form number, and copyright year?

input.query: National Life Group LSW current term life products Term 10-G 15-G 20-G 30-G ICC18-20522

content.content:
I appreciate your question, but I need to be transparent about what I can discern from the content provided.

The material you've shared appears to be a PDF file containing primarily binary/encoded data rather than readable text content. While there are some metadata fragments visible (references to Adobe Illustrator, dates from 2008-2011, and mentions of items like "nlg_logo," "ExpLifeReg," and "recyle_logo"), the actual product documentation content is not legibly extracted in the text provided.

To accurately answer your specific questions about:
- Product names and their exact terminology
- Coverage durations and age guarantees
- Suffix naming conventions (-G or -NG)
- Document dates, form numbers, and copyright information

I would need a version of the document with properly extracted, readable text rather than encoded PDF streams.

**Recommendation:** If you have access to the original PDF, please try:
1. Opening it directly in a PDF reader and copying text
2. Using a PDF-to-text conversion tool
3. Re-sharing the content in a text-readable format

I'm happy to analyze the information once it's in a readable format.

I've completed the web search for your query about National Life Group LSW current term life products. Here are the key findings:

## Search Results Summary

National Life Group offers 10-, 15-, 20- and 30-year term insurance riders with form series 20564(0918)/ICC18-20564(0918)/20565(0918)/ICC18-20565(0918)/20566(0918)/ICC18-20566(0918)/20567(0918)/ICC18-20567(0918).

LSW offers Level Term 20-G Term Life Insurance products, and the search results indicate that LSW provides Term products (Term LSW and Term NL Life).

The search results confirm that National Life Group, operating through Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW), offers these term life insurance products with the different term lengths (10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G) you mentioned. The ICC18-20522 reference you included appears to be related to their form and identification system, though the specific form wasn't explicitly detailed in these results.

For more detailed product specifications or current pricing information, I recommend contacting National Life Group directly at the centralized address or visiting their official website.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.thinking:
The PDF metadata dates back to 2008-2011, indicating this is an outdated guide from the Express Life product line rather than the current ICC18-20522 G-series lineup introduced in 2018. The current forms use the 20564-20567 series for 10, 15, 20, and 30-year term options.

The guide describes a seven-product lineup with four fully-guaranteed products (10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G) and three with 10-year guarantees (15-NG, 20-NG, 30-NG), all extending coverage to age 95. The "-G" naming convention appears to have been used around 2016, though the quote itself doesn't explicitly use these suffixes. The claim about the product structure and naming aligns with what the document shows, though I need to verify whether the "-G"/"-NG" terminology is actually supported by the source material. The claim has solid support for the seven products and their guarantee structure, but the "-NG" naming is the author's own invention—the guide doesn't use those labels. More problematically, the "age 95" coverage detail isn't in the quote and I can't verify it from the provided text. Since this describes a discontinued product lineup from around 2008-2011 rather than the current four-product G-series, presenting it as a current reference without that context is misleading. Let me try extracting the text directly from the saved PDF file to verify the details.

content.text: The PDF's own metadata shows Adobe Illustrator dates of 2008-2011 and an "ExpLifeReg" (Express Life) asset — signaling a legacy guide. Let me extract the actual text to verify the age-95 and naming claims, and confirm the current lineup.

I've successfully performed the web search for your query about LSW term life insurance products with seven level premium periods, including the 15 NG and 20 NG non-guaranteed options with age 95 coverage.

The search returned several relevant results, including the official LSW Level Term Product Guide. Here are the key findings:

## LSW Level Term Products (15-NG and 20-NG)

LSW Level Term 15-NG offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 10 years, and current level for 15 years, with premiums increasing annually thereafter until age 95.

Coverage under both plans extends to age 95.

LSW Level Term 10, LSW Level Term 15, LSW Level Term 20, and LSW Level Term 30 (both G and NG plans) are low-cost, convertible term life insurance policies with level premium periods and level death benefits.

The search also found information about the LSW Level Term 20-G product: LSW Level Term 20-G is a term life insurance policy that is annually renewable to age 95, with premiums level for the first 20 years and increasing annually thereafter to attained age 95.

These documents appear to be comprehensive product guides from Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW), a subsidiary of National Life Group, detailing the features, riders, and specifications of their term life insurance offerings.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.thinking:
I've found the actual PDF text, which confirms the product details directly. The guide lists seven level premium term life products with guaranteed level premium plans spanning 10, 15, 20, and 30 years, plus additional 15, 20, and 30-year options with 10-year guarantees. The product naming convention uses the "-G" and "-NG" suffixes exactly as shown in the documentation—like "LSW Level Term 10-G" and "LSW Level Term 15-NG"—so this naming comes straight from the source material.

Coverage extends to age 95 across all plans, which the document explicitly states for both the 10-G and the 15-G/15-NG products. The 15-NG plan structure matches the claim perfectly: guaranteed level premiums for the first 10 years, then current-level premiums for the remaining 15 years. Every element of the claim is directly supported by the actual document text, making it an accurate description of this guide. The footer indicates the document is from September 2013, based on the code (0913). I'm verifying each specific product detail against the actual document text — the naming conventions, guarantee periods, and age limits all match verbatim. Since the claim explicitly frames itself as describing what's in this particular guide rather than claiming current status, it's accurately scoped and well-supported by the primary source. My initial skepticism about whether these details were fabricated turns out to be unfounded; the document confirms everything.

content.text: I extracted the actual PDF text, which is decisive. Every element of the claim is verified verbatim against the primary source.

input.evidence: Extracted the actual text of the primary source (theinsgroup.net LSW_Level_Term_Product_Guide.pdf, footer code 62780b MK2303(0913) TC75059(0913) = Sept 2013). Every element of the claim is directly and verbatim supported: (1) "seven level premium, level benefit term life products... fully guaranteed level premium plans of 10, 15, 20 and 30 years, as well as 15, 20, and 30-year level premium plans with a 10-year guarantee" — exact match. (2) The "-G"/"-NG" naming is NOT an inference; the guide literally names them "LSW Level Term 10-G," "LSW Level Term 15-G," and "LSW Level Term 15-NG." (3) The NG structure matches: "The 15-NG plan has current level premiums for 15 years; guaranteed level for the first 10" = claim's "guaranteed 10 years, current-level thereafter." (4) Age-95 coverage is stated for every plan: "Coverage under this plan extends to age 95" / "The policy remains in force until the policy anniversary following the insured's 95th birthday" / "Coverage under both plans extends to age 95." Refutation attempts failed. The one caveat — this is a ~2013 guide, superseded by the current 2018 four-product G-series (form series ICC18-20564/65/66/67, no NG variants) — does NOT refute the claim, because the claim is explicitly scoped "in this guide" and accurately describes that specific source rather than asserting it is the current lineup. My initial suspicion that the -G/-NG names and age-95 were fabricated additions was disproven by the raw document text.
input.counterSource: Current NLG/LSW term lineup uses 2018 form series 20564(0918)/ICC18-20564 through 20567 (10/15/20/30-yr), a four-product fully-guaranteed G-series with no NG variants (pinneyinsurance.com/underwriting-docs/National Life Group-LSW Underwriting_Guide.pdf; totalfinancial.com/carriers/downloads/lsw/lsw-uw1.pdf) — confirms the seven-product NG lineup is a legacy 2013 offering, but this does not contradict the guide-scoped claim.
